visual_checks: Look for application-style interfaces (web apps, email clients, document editors, dashboards) that commonly implement single-character keyboard shortcuts
Check for visible keyboard shortcut hints, tooltips, or help overlays that list single-key bindings (e.g., 'Press S to save', 'Press ? for help')
Look for settings or preferences panels that might include options to remap or disable keyboard shortcuts
Identify rich web applications (Slack-like chat, Trello-like boards, Gmail-like interfaces) that typically use single-character shortcuts
Look for documentation or help sections mentioning keyboard shortcuts
Check for gaming or interactive content that may bind letter/number keys to actions
Look for any visible evidence that pressing a single character key (without modifiers like Ctrl, Alt, Shift) triggers an action
pass_conditions: No single-character keyboard shortcuts exist on the page — all shortcuts require a modifier key (Ctrl, Alt, Cmd, Shift)
Single-character shortcuts that exist can be turned off through a documented setting or preference
Single-character shortcuts that exist can be remapped by the user to include a modifier key
Single-character shortcuts only activate when the relevant UI component has focus (e.g., arrow keys in a listbox, letter keys in a search field with focus)
The page provides a mechanism to disable or reconfigure any character key shortcuts that are present
fail_conditions: Single-character keyboard shortcuts (letter, number, punctuation, or symbol keys) trigger actions regardless of focus position and cannot be turned off or remapped
Pressing a letter key outside of a text input triggers navigation, deletion, or other actions with no way to disable this behavior
The application uses single-key shortcuts globally (e.g., 'j' for next item, 'k' for previous) with no option to turn them off or remap them
Speech recognition users or users with motor impairments experience unintended actions from accidental single-key presses because shortcuts cannot be disabled
na_conditions: Page is a standard content page with no application-like keyboard shortcuts — only standard browser and native HTML element shortcuts apply
Page contains only standard forms, links, and content with no custom JavaScript keyboard shortcut bindings
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Cannot determine whether single-character shortcuts exist from screenshots and tab walk data alone — JavaScript event listeners are not visible in the captured data
Page functionality could not be fully exercised to test for character key shortcuts
common_mistakes: Web applications copying desktop app conventions (e.g., 'j/k' for navigation in email clients) without providing a way to disable them
Single-character shortcuts that work globally even when the user is typing in a text field, causing unintended actions
Keyboard shortcut implementations that offer no settings panel or toggle to disable them
Custom media players using Space, K, M, F as global shortcuts without requiring the player to have focus first
Shortcut help dialogs that reveal single-key shortcuts exist but no option to remap or disable them
Game-like interfaces binding letter keys to actions without a modifier key requirement

examples.pass: A web email client uses 'J' and 'K' to navigate messages but provides a Settings > Keyboard Shortcuts page where users can disable all shortcuts or remap them to require Ctrl as a modifier.
examples.fail: A project management board uses single keys — 'N' for new card, 'D' for delete, 'E' for edit — globally on the page. There is no way to turn these off. A speech recognition user accidentally deletes items when voice input is misrecognized as keystrokes.
examples.partial: The application provides single-character shortcuts and has a help dialog listing them, but the option to disable shortcuts is buried in an inaccessible settings page that itself requires mouse interaction to reach.

plain_meaning: If the page implements keyboard shortcuts using only letter, number, punctuation, or symbol characters (no modifier keys), the user must be able to turn the shortcut off, remap it, or have it active only when the target control has focus. The intent is to prevent accidental activation by speech-input users and others whose typing may unexpectedly fire shortcuts.
scope_applies_to: Single-character shortcuts bound to page actions (pressing a letter to go to next item)
Single-character shortcuts bound to navigation, search, or command palettes
Punctuation or symbol shortcuts (a slash to focus search) active globally on the page
Number-key shortcuts bound to global actions
scope_does_not_apply_to: Shortcuts that require a modifier key (Ctrl, Alt, Shift, Meta/Cmd) in combination
Shortcuts that are active only while a specific control has focus (e.g. arrow keys in a listbox)
Native form-control behavior (typing in a text field, arrow keys inside a select)
Access keys that require a modifier per browser convention
Pages that implement no custom keyboard shortcuts at all
borderline_calibration: If no single-character shortcut exists on the page, this SC is N/A.
A shortcut scoped to a focused widget (not the whole page) automatically satisfies the active-only-on-focus condition.
Presence of Ctrl/Alt/Shift/Meta in the key combination means out of scope -- do not flag.
The page need only offer ONE of turn-off/remap/focus-scope, not all three.
